summary: I was born and raised in Cuba, where life was really about survival. Salaries were extremely low, like making 10 pesos a day while basic food items cost much more. There was no freedom of expression, and owning a business was not allowed since everyone had to work for the government. I left Cuba in 2006 searching for fair pay and freedom, but it was bittersweet because my parents had to stay behind for a long time.

I was born in Havana, the capital of Cuba, and spent my childhood there. Despite the hardships, my parents made sure I had a good upbringing. Havana was where I first experienced the struggles of low wages and limited freedoms under the government. Leaving Havana and Cuba in 2006 was a big step for me, hoping to find a better life in the United States.

I came to the United States in 2006 full of hope for freedom and fair pay. The day I got my visa was one of the best days of my life, even though my parents were initially denied because of their age. After a year, my sister was able to bring my parents here, and they lived happily until they passed away. The respect and kindness I found here were unlike anything I had known in Cuba, and I thank God every day for living in this country.
